sentence: Subjective:

The patient presented with a three-month history of an intermittent cough that started after a physically demanding activity, initially thought to be a cold, but was diagnosed as bronchitis by their primary care physician.
They were referred to me for further evaluation of a lung nodule found on a chest X-ray and confirmed by a CT scan.
The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
They are a former smoker, quitting about seven years ago.
They have a family history of breast cancer, but no history of lung cancer or other malignancies.
They have no previous history of lung infections or pneumonia.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any pain, shortness of breath, or other symptoms.
Current medications:
I prescribed methotrexate 7.5mg weekly for rheumatoid arthritis.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 124/76, heart rate is 70, respiratory rate is 16, oxygen saturation on room air is 98%, and the patient is afebrile.
Physical exam: The patient's lungs are clear and equal bilateral when auscultated, with no wheezes or rales.
The heart is regular in rate and rhythm, with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs.
The extremities have normal skin temperature and moisture, with no edema.
There is some swelling of the knuckles on both hands, which is likely related to the patient's rheumatoid arthritis.
Test Results:
The patient had a CT scan that showed a solitary 2cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which appears smooth and without evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule that is being further evaluated with a PET CT and pulmonary function tests.
Problem list: Lung nodule confirmed; active rheumatoid arthritis; history of smoking.
Plan:
I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for lung function.
I will also schedule a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
I will suggest video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule, which will be performed under general anesthesia, and the patient will be admitted for one night and discharged the following day.
The patient will continue to follow up with their rheumatologist and maintain their current medication therapy.
Follow-up:
The patient will be seen again in a few minutes to sign the necessary paperwork and begin the treatment process.
